task: Rebuilt literature summary v3: event camera object detection/tracking (2021-present)

hard_constraints: must include explicit object detection/tracking semantics + evaluation evidence
exclude non-target tasks (SLAM, pose estimation, super-resolution, optical flow, eye/star tracking, astronomy, device reviews)

selected_papers.main_content: We present Recurrent Vision Transformers (RVTs), a novel backbone for object detection with event cameras. Event cameras provide visual information with submillisecond latency at a high-dynamic range and with strong robustness against motion blur. selected_papers.main_content: Neuromorphic vision sensors, whose pixels output events/spikes asynchronously with a high temporal resolution according to the scene radiance change, are naturally appropriate for capturing high-speed motion in the scenes. selected_papers.main_content: The development of aerial autonomy has enabled aerial robots to fly agilely in complex environments. However, dodging fast-moving objects in flight remains a challenge, limiting the further application of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). Traditional frame cameras processed through convolutional neural networks (CNN) exhibit high target-identification accuracy but with low throughput (hence low tracking speed) and high power. selected_papers.title: Spike-Event Object Detection for Neuromorphic Vision

selected_papers.main_content: Neuromorphic vision is one of the novel research fields that study neuromorphic cameras and spiking neural networks (SNNs) for computer vision. Instead of computing on frame-based images, spike events are streamed from neuromorphic cameras, and novel object detection algorithms have to deal with spike events to achieve detection tasks. In this paper, we propose a solution to the novel object detection method with spi

selected_papers.title: SpikingViT: A Multiscale Spiking Vision Transformer Model for Event-Based Object Detection

selected_papers.main_content: Event cameras have unique advantages in object detection, capturing asynchronous events without continuous frames. They excel in dynamic range, low latency, and high-speed motion scenarios, with lower power consumption. However, aggregating event data into image frames leads to information loss and reduced detection performance. Applying traditional neural networks to event camera outputs is challenging due to event

selected_papers.main_content: While recent Transformer-based approaches have shown impressive performances on event-based object detection tasks, their high computational costs still diminish the low power consumption advantage of event cameras. selected_papers.title: Pushing the Limits of Asynchronous Graph-based Object Detection with Event Cameras

selected_papers.main_content: State-of-the-art machine-learning methods for event cameras treat events as dense representations and process them with conventional deep neural networks. Thus, they fail to maintain the sparsity and asynchronous nature of event data, thereby imposing significant computation and latency constraints on downstream systems. selected_papers.main_content: To enhance the accuracy of object detection with event-based neuromorphic vision sensors, a novel event-based detector named Spatio-Temporal Aggregation Transformer (STAT) is proposed. Firstly, in order to collect sufficient event information to estimate the problem considered, STAT uses a density-based adaptive sampling (DAS) module to sample continuous event stream into multiple groups adaptively. selected_papers.title: LEOD: Label-Efficient Object Detection for Event Cameras
selected_papers.selection_reason: 内容含数据集/benchmark证据；内容含评估指标/实验结果语句；摘要级验证
selected_papers.main_content: Object detection with event cameras benefits from the sensor's low latency and high dynamic range. However, it is costly to fully label event streams for supervised training due to their high temporal resolution. To reduce this cost, we present LEOD, the first method for label-efficient event-based detection. Our approach unifies weakly- and semi-supervised object detection with a self-training mech-anism.
